volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Rope (Section 3.6.855 - Section 4.3.945)

sections.label: Section 3.6.855
sections.original: Abi sáne ad litus curriculo , Trachalio ,
sections.translation: Be off then , Trachalio , with all speed to the sea-shore ; bid those persons that I brought with me to hand over this rascal to the executioner , to come from the harbour to the city to meet me ; afterwards return hither and keep guard here . I ’ ll now drag this scoundrelly outcast to justice . ( Exit TRACHALIO . Pleusicles addresses LABRAX .)

sections.label: Section 3.6.860
sections.translation: Come , proceed to a court of justice .

sections.label: Section 3.6.860b
sections.translation: In what have I offended ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.860c
sections.translation: Do you ask ? Didn ’ t you receive an earnest of me for this woman ( pointing to PALAESTRA ) , and carry her off from here ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.862b
sections.translation: I didn ’ t carry her off .

sections.label: Section 3.6.862c
sections.translation: Why do you deny it ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.863
sections.original: Quia pol provexi : avehere non quivi miser .
sections.translation: Troth now , because I put her on board ship ; carry her off [*] unfortunately , I couldn ’ t . For my part , I told you that this day I would make my appearance

sections.label: Section 3.6.865
sections.original: apud Véneris fanum : num quid muto ? sumne ibi ?
sections.translation: at the Temple of Venus ; have I swerved at all from that ? Am I not there ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.866
sections.original: In iure causam dicito , hic verbum sat est :
sections.translation: Plead your cause in the court of justice ; here a word is enough . Follow me .

sections.label: Section 3.6.867b
sections.translation: I entreat you , my dear Charmides , do come to my rescue ; I am being seized with my throat wrenched .

sections.label: Section 3.6.868b
sections.translation: Who calls my name ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.869
sections.translation: Do you see me how I ’ m being seized ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.869b
sections.translation: I see , and view it with pleasure .

sections.label: Section 3.6.870
sections.translation: Don ’ t you venture to assist me ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.870b
sections.translation: What person is seizing you ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.871
sections.translation: Young Plesidippus .

sections.label: Section 3.6.871b
sections.translation: What you ’ ve got , put up with ; ’ twere better for you , with a cheerful spirit , to slink to gaol ; that has befallen you which many greatly wish for for themselves .

sections.label: Section 3.6.874
sections.translation: What ’ s that ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.874b
sections.original: Vt id quod quaerant inveniant sibi .
sections.translation: To find for themselves that which they are seeking .

sections.label: Section 3.6.875
sections.translation: I entreat you , do follow me .

sections.label: Section 3.6.875b
sections.translation: You try to persuade me , just like what you are : you are being taken off to gaol , for that reason is it you entreat me to follow you ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.877
sections.translation: Do you still resist ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.877b
sections.translation: I ’ m undone .

sections.label: Section 3.6.877c
sections.translation: I trust that may prove the truth . You , my dear Palaestra and Ampelisca , do you remain here in the meanwhile , until I return hither .

sections.label: Section 3.6.879b
sections.translation: I would advise them

sections.label: Section 3.6.880
sections.original: ut ad nos abeant potius , dum recipis .
sections.translation: rather to go to our house , until you return .

sections.label: Section 3.6.880b
sections.translation: I ’ m quite agreable ; you act obligingly .

sections.label: Section 3.6.881b
sections.translation: You are thieves to me .

sections.label: Section 3.6.881c
sections.translation: How , thieves ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.882
sections.translation: I pray and entreat you , Palaestra .

sections.label: Section 3.6.882b
sections.translation: Follow , you hang-dog .

sections.label: Section 3.6.883
sections.translation: Guest , Charmides !

sections.label: Section 3.6.883b
sections.original: Non sum hospes , repudio hospitium tuom .
sections.translation: I am no guest of yours ; I repudiate your hospitality .

sections.label: Section 3.6.884
sections.translation: What , do you slight me in this fashion ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.884b
sections.translation: I do so ; I ’ ve been drinking with you once already [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.6.885
sections.translation: May the Deities confound you .

sections.label: Section 3.6.885b
sections.translation: To that person of yours , say that . ( PLESIDIPPUS leads LABRAX off , followed by the SERVANTS .) I do believe that men are transformed , each into a different beast . That Procurer , I guess , is transformed into a stock-dove [*] ; for , before long , his neck will be in the stocks . He ’ ll to-day be building his nest in the gaol .

sections.label: Section 3.6.890
sections.original: verum tamen ibo , ei advocatus ut siem ,
sections.translation: Still , however , I ’ ll go , that I may be his advocate ,— if by my aid he may possibly be sentenced any the sooner .

sections.label: Section 4.1.892
sections.original: Bene factum et volup est , mé hodie his mulierculis
sections.translation: ’ Twas rightly done , and it is a pleasure this day for me to have given aid to these young women ; I have now found some dependants , and both of them of comely looks and youthful age .

sections.label: Section 4.1.895
sections.original: sed uxór scelesta me omnibus servat modis ,
sections.translation: But my plaguy wife is watching me in all ways , lest I should be giving any hint to the young women . But I wonder what in the world my servant Gripus is about , who went last night to the sea to fish . Troth , he had done wiser if he had slept at home ;

sections.label: Section 4.1.900
sections.original: nam nunc et operam ludos facit et retia ,
sections.translation: for now he throws away both his pains and his nets , seeing what a storm there now is and was last night . I ’ ll thoroughly cook upon my fingers what he has caught to-day ; so violently do I see the ocean heaving . ( A bell rings .) But my wife ’ s calling me to breakfast ; I ’ ll return home .

sections.label: Section 4.1.905
sections.original: iam meas opplebit aures † sua vaniloquentia .—
sections.translation: She ’ ll now be filling my ears with her silly prating .

sections.label: Section 4.2.906
sections.original: Neptúno has agó gratiás meo patróno ,
sections.translation: These thanks do I return to Neptune , my patron , who dwells in the salt retreats , the abode of fishes , inasmuch as he has despatched me finely laden on my return from his retreats , and from his Temples , laden with most abundant booty ,

sections.label: Section 4.2.910
sections.original: salúte horiaé , quae in marí fluctuóso
sections.translation: with safety to my boat , which in the stormy sea made me master of a singular and rich haul . In a wondrous and incredible manner has this haul turned out prosperously for me , nor yet have I this day taken a single ounce weight of fish , but only that which I am here bringing with me in my net .

sections.label: Section 4.2.914_915
sections.original: nam ut dé nocte múlta impigréque exurréxi ,
sections.translation: For when I arose in the middle of the night , and without sloth , I preferred profit to sleep and rest ; in the raging tempest , I determined to try how I might lighten the poverty of my master and my own servitude , not sparing of my own exertions .

sections.label: Section 4.2.920
sections.original: nímis homo nihilist quis piger est nímisque id genus odi égo male .
sections.translation: Most worthless is the man that is slothful , and most detestably do I hate that kind of men . It behoves him to be vigilant who wishes to do his duty in good time ; for it befits him not to be waiting until his master arouses him to his duties . For those who sleep on for the love of it , rest without profit to themselves and to their own cost .

sections.label: Section 4.2.924
sections.original: nám ego nunc mihi , qui ímpiger fui ,
sections.translation: But now I , who have not been slothful , have found that for myself through which to be slothful if I should choose .

sections.label: Section 4.2.925a
sections.original: hóc ego in mari quidquid inést répperi .
sections.translation: ( Points to the wallet .) This have I found in the sea to-day ; whatever ’ s in it , it ’ s something heavy that ’ s in it ; I think it ’ s gold that ’ s in it . And not a single person is there my confidant in the matter . Now , Gripus , this opportunity has befallen you , that the Praetor [*] might make you a free man from among the multitude . Now , thus shall I do , this is my determination ; I ’ ll come to my master cleverly and cunningly , little by little I ’ ll promise money for my freedom , that I may be free .

sections.label: Section 4.2.930
sections.original: iam ubi líber ero , igitúr demum instrúam agrum atque aedis , máncipia ,
sections.translation: Now , when I shall be free , then , in fine , I ’ ll provide me land and houses [*] and slaves : I ’ ll carry on merchandize with large ships : among the grandees I shall be considered a grandee . Afterwards , for the sake of pleasing myself , I ’ ll build me a ship and I ’ ll imitate Stratonicus [*] , and I ’ ll be carried about from town to town . When my greatness is far-spread , I shall fortify some great city : to that city I shall give the name of Gripus ,

sections.label: Section 4.2.935
sections.original: moniméntum meae famae ét factis , ibi quí regnum magnum ínstituam .
sections.translation: a memorial of my fame and exploits , and there I ’ ll establish a mighty kingdom . I am resolving here in my mind to prepare for mighty matters . At present I ’ ll hide this booty . But this grandee ( pointing to himself ) is about to breakfast upon vinegar [*] and salt , without any good substantial meat .

sections.label: Section 4.3.938
sections.translation: Hallo there ! stop .

sections.label: Section 4.3.938b
sections.translation: Why should I stop ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.938c
sections.original: Dum hánc tibi , quam tráhis , rudentem cómplico .
sections.translation: While I coil up this rope [*] for you that you are dragging .

sections.label: Section 4.3.939
sections.translation: Now let it alone .

sections.label: Section 4.3.939b
sections.original: At pol ego te ádiuvo nam bónis quod bene fit haúd perit .
sections.translation: Troth , but I ’ ll assist you . What ’ s kindly done to worthy men , isn ’ t thrown away .

sections.label: Section 4.3.940
sections.translation: ⋯ There was a boisterous tempest yesterday ; no fish have I , young man ; don ’ t you be supposing I have . Don ’ t you see that I ’ m carrying my dripping net without the scaly race ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.943
sections.original: Non édepol piscis éxpeto quam tuí sermonis sum índigens .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I ’ m not wishing for fish so much as I am in need of your conversation .

sections.label: Section 4.3.944
sections.original: Enícas iam me odio , quísquis es .
sections.translation: Then , whoever you are , you are worrying me to death with your annoyance .

sections.label: Section 4.3.944b
sections.original: Non sínam ego abire hinc té . mane .
sections.translation: I ’ ll not allow you to go away from here ; stop .

sections.label: Section 4.3.945
sections.original: Cave sís malo . quid tú , malum , nám me retrahis ?
sections.translation: Take you care of a mishap , if you please ; but why the plague are you dragging me back ?
